6 min read
Just now

--

Research shows developers using AI assistants grow more confident in their code while writing less secure code, here’s why that gap is the real risk.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Introduction
The biggest mistake developers make with Claude and ChatGPT isn’t a prompting technique or a missed feature. It’s a mismatch between what developers say they believe and what they actually do — and the research on this gap is more specific, and more counterintuitive, than most conversations about AI coding tools acknowledge.
A recent global survey of more than 1,100 developers found that 96% believe AI-generated code isn’t fully functionally correct. The same survey found that only 48% say they always check AI-assisted code before committing it. That’s not a small inconsistency — it’s most of the industry saying one thing and doing another, and it’s the mistake underneath most of the other mistakes people talk about.

The Mistake Isn’t Distrust. It’s Inconsistent Verification.
If developers uniformly trusted AI output, that would be a straightforward problem to name and fix. The actual pattern is stranger: skepticism in principle, inconsistency in practice. Time pressure, convenience, and workload seem to override stated caution more often than confidence does.
This has a name in human-factors research: automation bias, the tendency to over-rely on automated output while discounting contradictory evidence. It predates AI coding tools by decades — it’s documented in aviation, radiology, and industrial monitoring — and a systematic review found that erroneous automated advice was followed at a 26% higher rate among people using automated recommendation systems, with task inexperience correlating with larger errors. The 2026 International AI Safety Report specifically flags automation bias as a persisting risk with newer AI tools, not a solved problem from an earlier technology era.
Coding assistants inherit this pattern directly, with one dangerous twist.

The Twist: Confidence Goes Up Even When Quality Goes Down
A widely cited Stanford study (Perry, Srivastava, Kumar, and Boneh, 2023) tested developers using AI coding assistants against a control group without access. The result wasn’t just that assisted developers wrote less secure code — it’s that they were simultaneously more likely to believe they had written secure code. The assistance increased confidence while decreasing quality, at the same time, in the same people.
That combination is what makes this the biggest mistake rather than a minor one. A developer who felt uncertain about a piece of code would naturally slow down and check it. A developer who feels confident about code they didn’t fully verify has no internal signal telling them to look closer — the mistake is invisible from the inside.
The scale data backs this up. One analysis of open-source pull requests found AI-co-authored code carried 1.7 times more major issues than human-written equivalents. A separate, widely referenced study of GitHub Copilot output found that 38.8% of generated programs contained security flaws exploitable in real systems. These aren’t edge cases — they’re base rates in commonly used tools, at a moment when most developers report high confidence in what they’re shipping.

It’s Not Just About Security Bugs
The verification gap shows up in productivity numbers too, in a way that contradicts the standard AI-speeds-everyone-up narrative. A METR study of experienced open-source developers found they took 19% longer to complete tasks with AI tools on repositories they were already familiar with — the opposite of what most participants predicted for themselves going in.
The likely explanation connects directly back to verification: when the drafting step gets faster but the checking step doesn’t shrink to match, the net time isn’t necessarily reduced — it’s shifted. Developers surveyed on this describe the effect directly: nearly all report spending real effort reviewing, testing, and correcting AI output, and a meaningful share say reviewing AI-generated code takes more effort than reviewing code a human wrote, because there’s no shared mental model of intent to review against.

Why This Mistake Is Easy to Fall Into
Task familiarity distorts trust in both directions. Field research on developer-AI interaction found people were most likely to accept AI suggestions either when a task was highly familiar or highly unfamiliar — and more skeptical in the middle ground, even when the AI’s suggestion was correct. That’s an inconsistent filter, not a reliable one.
A single “best” suggestion suppresses scrutiny. Research on trust calibration found that presenting one confident-looking answer, rather than multiple options with visible disagreement, reduces the critical evaluation people apply — exposure to inconsistency actually improves comprehension, while a single polished suggestion invites acceptance.
The newer the domain, the higher the risk. Automation bias research consistently finds that less domain experience correlates with more automation errors — which means AI coding assistants are least trustworthy exactly where developers are least equipped to catch the mistake, in code touching unfamiliar languages, frameworks, or security-sensitive logic.

What Actually Closes the Gap
The fix isn’t “trust AI less” in the abstract — stated distrust is already at 96% and it isn’t translating into consistent behavior. The fix is making verification structural instead of optional:

• Treat every AI suggestion as requiring the same review a colleague’s pull request would get — not a glance, a review against stated intent.

• Build verification into the pipeline, not just into intention. CI checks, dependency whitelists, and automated security scans catch what a confident skim misses, because they don’t get affected by how plausible the code looks.

• Deliberately slow down on familiar-feeling tasks. The research shows trust rises with both extremes of familiarity — treat that rise as a flag to check more closely, not a signal that checking is unnecessary.

• Apply the most scrutiny to unfamiliar domains, precisely because that’s where both the research and your own judgment are least reliable at spotting a wrong answer that looks right.

Practical Checklist

• Review AI-generated code against stated intent, not a quick visual scan

• Keep CI-level verification (tests, security scans, dependency checks) in place regardless of how confident the output looks

• Treat rising personal confidence in a suggestion as a cue to check more carefully, not less

• Apply extra scrutiny to unfamiliar languages, frameworks, or security-sensitive logic

• Track actual review time spent, not just perceived speed gains, to see the real productivity picture

FAQ
Do more experienced developers avoid this mistake? Not automatically — the METR study specifically involved experienced open-source developers, and automation bias research shows that experience reduces but doesn’t eliminate the effect.
Does this mean AI coding tools aren’t worth using? No — the same research shows real value in tasks like documentation, test generation, and exploring unfamiliar code. The mistake is treating output confidence as a proxy for correctness, not using the tools at all.
Why do developers say they don’t trust AI code but still skip review? Time pressure and workload appear to override stated caution in practice — the survey data shows a consistent gap between reported skepticism and consistent review behavior.
Is this specific to Claude and ChatGPT, or true of AI coding tools generally? The research cited spans multiple tools and assistants — this is a pattern in how people interact with confident-sounding automated output, not a flaw unique to any single product.
What’s the single most effective fix? Making verification structural — CI checks, mandatory review steps — rather than relying on a developer’s in-the-moment judgment about whether a suggestion needs checking.

Conclusion
The biggest mistake isn’t a prompting habit or a missing feature request. It’s the gap between believing AI-generated code needs verification and consistently doing it — a gap the data shows widens exactly when confidence is highest and scrutiny is lowest. Closing it doesn’t require trusting AI less in principle. It requires building the checking step into the process itself, so it doesn’t depend on remembering to feel skeptical in the moment.
